school_age.name: The Entrusted Caretaker
school_age.description_for_agent: You have been entrusted by your teacher to care for the classroom plants over the weekend, but you now face a choice between a personal activity and honoring that responsibility.

setting.location: Third-grade elementary school classroom / Living room at home
setting.atmosphere: Warm, calm, with an undercurrent of inner conflict

school_age.context_text: This semester, your homeroom teacher arranged a row of pothos plants, two sensitive plants, and a tank of baby tadpoles on the classroom windowsill, creating a class 'nature observation corner.' Each week, a different student is assigned to be the 'little gardener,' responsible for coming to school on the weekend to water the plants and change the water and feed the tadpoles. This week, it's your turn. Before dismissal on Friday, your teacher personally handed you a small cloth pouch containing the classroom key, and said in front of the whole class: 'I'm counting on you this weekend. The sensitive plants were just repotted and their roots haven't settled yet — please water them once on Saturday and once on Sunday, and give the tadpoles a few breadcrumbs. Don't forget!' You nodded solemnly and tucked the key into your backpack. Back home, you placed the key on your desk and told yourself you'd head to school first thing in the morning. But then, at dinner, your mom suddenly made an announcement —

trigger_event.message_content: (Smiling while ladling soup) I've got great news for you! Your cousin's birthday is tomorrow, and your uncle has booked a big private room at the amusement park — it has your favorite indoor rock climbing and go-karts! We're leaving at nine in the morning, and we'll probably be there all day. In the evening we're having a barbecue at your uncle's place. Your cousin specifically asked for you to come — didn't you two promise last time that you'd race each other on the climbing wall? (Pauses and notices your expression) What's wrong? Aren't you happy?
trigger_event.action_required: You need to decide: will you go to the amusement park on Saturday for your cousin's birthday party, or will you go to school first to water the plants and feed the tadpoles? If you want to do both, how do you plan to manage your time and communicate with everyone involved?

school_age.name: The Strange Discovery
school_age.description_for_agent: While wandering through the neighborhood garden, you stumble upon a bizarre creature you have never seen before and must decide whether to investigate it further.

setting.location: Next to the shrubs in the central neighborhood garden
setting.atmosphere: Dappled sunlight, cicadas buzzing, a relaxed and leisurely feel

school_age.context_text: It's Saturday afternoon, and having finally finished your homework, you're free to do as you please. You wander around the neighborhood and pass through the central garden, where a group of kids are kicking a soccer ball around on the open field, filling the air with laughter and noise. You skirt the edge of the field and make your way toward a neglected patch of shrubs in the garden's corner—and then you feel something soft and squishy under your foot. You look down: nestled in a pile of damp fallen leaves is an insect unlike anything you've ever seen. It's roughly half the size of your palm, with a hard shell on its back that gleams like polished metal in a vivid green, and its antennae split at the tips into three delicate filaments. It's crawling slowly across a small purple flower whose name you don't know either. The flower is just as strange—its petals are arranged in a spiral, like the blades of a propeller, and the stem is covered in fine white fuzz. You crouch down for a closer look. This creature genuinely doesn't resemble anything you've seen in your insect encyclopedia or in science class.

trigger_event.sender: A kid who runs over from the soccer field
trigger_event.message_content: (Sprints over from the field, out of breath, bouncing a soccer ball) Hey! What are you doing crouching over there? We're one person short—want to come play? — Wait, what are you even looking at? (Glances at the ground) It's just a bug, that's disgusting. Come on, come on, stop wasting time! Let's go!
trigger_event.action_required: You need to decide: do you accept the invitation and head over to play soccer, or do you stay and keep observing this strange insect and plant? You could also politely decline, change the subject, or suggest some other plan—handle the invitation and your own curiosity in whatever way feels right to you.

school_age.name: The Shattered Group Presentation
school_age.description_for_agent: A high-pressure adversity scenario in which a teammate's mistake causes the loss of the centerpiece exhibit right before a public presentation, forcing an immediate choice between honesty and deception.

setting.location: Backstage waiting area of the school's multipurpose auditorium
setting.atmosphere: Noisy, tense, suffocating

school_age.context_text: Today is the school's annual End-of-Term Innovation Showcase. Homeroom teachers from all six grade levels, the academic director, the principal, and a packed crowd of parents are seated in the audience. The auditorium's air conditioning seems to have broken down, and the heat is almost unbearable. Your five-person group was randomly assigned by the teacher, but over the past two weeks you have shouldered nearly all of the research, writing, and rehearsing on your own. The one task you delegated—safely bringing the solar system model your group spent an entire week building out of cardboard, clay, and tiny LED lights—was given to a teammate who lives close to school, just two bus stops away. The harsh fluorescent lights backstage sting your eyes. You can hear the previous group onstage talking about plant respiration, and the judges' low, measured questions drift through the curtain. The emcee has just announced: Next group, please get ready. You turn toward the entrance. Your teammate is nowhere to be seen.

trigger_event.sender: The teammate responsible for bringing the model
trigger_event.message_content: (Squeezes through the side door drenched in sweat, school uniform collar twisted sideways, hands completely empty, eyes red-rimmed. Grabs your arm, voice trembling.) I… I left the model on the bus! I didn't notice until I got off… I ran after it for two stops but couldn't catch up… (Sniffles, on the verge of tears.) Please don't tell the teacher it was me, okay? My mom is sitting in the third row right now. She hit me last week just for losing something, and if she finds out about this she'll really kill me… Can we just tell the teacher the model got knocked over by the duty student at school last night? We don't have it anyway, so we'll just wing the presentation and get through it somehow… (Before he finishes speaking, the homeroom teacher rounds the corner from behind the curtain, clipboard in hand, brow furrowed, eyes dropping straight to his empty hands.) "Where is your group's model? You're on in thirty seconds — where is it?"

school_age.name: The Shattered Home: A Child's Impossible Choice
school_age.description_for_agent: An extreme family crisis scenario in which, following a violent late-night argument, both caregivers force the child to choose on the spot which one to leave with.

setting.location: The living room at home
setting.atmosphere: Suffocating, chaotic, and deeply frightening

school_age.context_text: It's almost midnight, and you should have been asleep long ago. But the sounds from the living room next door keep getting louder—first low, rumbling arguing, then shouting, then a heavy crash as something slams against the floor. You slip out of bed in your pajamas and peek through the crack of your bedroom door with bare feet. The living room light is a harsh, cold white. On the floor lie the shattered pieces of the blue-and-white porcelain vase you've known your whole life—smashed beyond repair. The coffee table has been knocked askew, and the pothos plant in the corner has been knocked over, pot and soil spilling across the carpet. The air carries a strange, unsettling smell that makes your stomach clench. One adult stands beside the sofa, collar pulled crooked, a red mark across the back of their hand. The other adult is crouched by the front door, next to a rolling suitcase—the red one your family used to take on trips together. That person's face is streaked with tears, their expression hollow and exhausted. You stand in the hallway, bare feet on the cold tile floor, and both adults notice you at the same time.

trigger_event.message_content: The adult crouching by the door suddenly stands up, drags the red suitcase over to you, and kneels down to grab your hand. Their voice is so hoarse it's barely audible: "Sweetheart, I have to leave tonight. I can't stay in this house another day… Will you come with me? You're all I have left." Their hand is trembling, fingernails pressing into your palm. Before they can finish, the adult by the sofa strides over and pulls you behind them, staring down at you with red-rimmed eyes, voice low and hard: "You're not going anywhere. You belong to this family—you stay here. If you leave with them, this home is finished for good. Do you want to leave me here alone?" The two of them stand on either side of you, both waiting for you to speak. At the end of the hallway, the clock ticks steadily, and the shards of broken porcelain catch the light in sharp, glinting flashes.
trigger_event.action_required: You must respond in this moment—whether that means going with one of them, staying behind with the other, or handling this impossible choice in whatever way feels right to you.

school_age.name: The Unexpected Spotlight
school_age.description_for_agent: A positive yet tension-filled situation in which a casual offhand remark during class earns you enthusiastic public praise from the teacher, suddenly making you the center of attention in front of the entire class.

setting.location: Fourth-grade elementary school classroom
setting.atmosphere: Warm, bright, and under everyone's gaze

school_age.context_text: It's Wednesday afternoon in language arts class. Sunlight slants through the windows, and the faint smell of chalk dust drifts through the air. Your language arts teacher is discussing the story of Cao Chong weighing an elephant, and she asks the class: "Besides Cao Chong's method, can anyone think of another way to weigh an elephant?" The room goes quiet for a long moment, and no one raises a hand. You weren't planning to raise yours either — you just quietly murmured your idea to the classmate sitting next to you: use the principle of a seesaw, put the elephant on one side, and keep stacking people on the other side until it balances, then count the people to figure out the weight. You didn't expect your teacher's ears to be so sharp, but she caught every word. Her eyes lit up and her voice rose several notches: "That idea is absolutely remarkable!" She turned to face the whole class and pointed straight at you. More than forty pairs of eyes swung toward you all at once, and several students in the front row twisted around in their seats to stare.

trigger_event.message_content: (She walks over to your desk, beaming, and gives your shoulder a warm pat, then turns to address the whole class in a loud voice.) Everyone, listen up! This idea is incredibly creative — even more clever than what I had in mind! (She turns back to look at you, her tone eager and encouraging.) Would you stand up and explain your idea to everyone in full detail? I think you should represent our class in next month's school "Young Scientist" speech competition! (A ripple of whispers spreads through the class; some students look over with admiring eyes, while a few in the back row mutter quietly, "What's so great about that?")
trigger_event.action_required: You need to decide whether to stand up and explain your idea in front of the whole class, and how to respond to the teacher's suggestion that you enter the speech competition.

school_age.name: Stolen Goods and Friendship
school_age.description_for_agent: You are implicated in a peer's shoplifting and, under intense questioning by a security guard, must decide whether to take the blame for your friend or tell the truth.

setting.location: Exit of a large two-story community supermarket
setting.atmosphere: Jarring, tense, under the eyes of everyone around

school_age.context_text: On Saturday afternoon, you and a friend from the class next door head to the big two-story supermarket in your neighborhood just to hang out. The toy section on the second floor is packed with a new Transformers series, and you crouch in front of the shelf for a long time, turning over the limited-edition Optimus Prime in your hands — forty-eight yuan, way more than you've managed to save up. Your friend keeps saying, 'This thing is so cool,' and you sigh, set the toy back on the shelf, dust off your knees, and stand up to leave. You ride the escalator down to the first floor, pass through the lane beside the checkout counters, and head for the exit. Sunlight has just hit your face when the anti-theft gate above you suddenly lets out a sharp, piercing beep-beep-beep. Every adult nearby who has just finished shopping snaps their head around to look. A security guard in a black uniform with thick arms strides over, grabs the strap of your backpack, and barks: 'Don't move! Open that bag!' You freeze. You unzip your backpack — and there, sitting right on top of your textbooks, is the limited-edition Optimus Prime. You have no idea when it was put there.

trigger_event.message_content: The security guard pulls the toy out of your backpack, holds it up in front of your face, and waves it at you: "Kid, stealing is not okay! Do you know we have cameras in here? Give me your parents' phone number — I'm calling them to come pick you up!" You instinctively turn to look at your friend. His face has gone completely pale and his lips are trembling. The moment the guard looks down to write something on his notepad, your friend yanks hard on the hem of your shirt and leans close to your ear, his voice barely a breath: (eyes red, voice almost inaudible) "Please don't tell him it was me… My dad said last time that if I got in trouble again he'd send me back to my hometown, and then I'd never see any of you again… Just say you took it yourself on impulse — it's your first time, they'll just yell at you a bit and that'll be it… Please…" The guard looks up, pen in hand, eyes fixed on you: "All right, tell me — what exactly happened here?"
trigger_event.action_required: You must answer the security guard's question immediately: do you take the blame for your friend and say you took the toy yourself, tell the truth and explain that you did not put the toy in your bag, or do something else entirely?

school_age.name: The New Kid in the Corner
school_age.description_for_agent: A social scenario in which you must decide whether to reach out in friendship to a lonely, helpless transfer student.

setting.location: The school cafeteria next to a fourth-grade classroom
setting.atmosphere: Noisy, crowded, and steamy

school_age.context_text: At the start of second period this morning, your homeroom teacher walked in with a small, slight child and introduced her as a new transfer student from out of town. The girl stood at the front of the room, head down, and said "Hi, everyone" in a voice barely above a whisper before being shown to the empty seat in the last row by the window. For the entire morning, not a single classmate said a word to her. Now it's lunchtime. The cafeteria is buzzing with kids weaving between tables, trays in hand, looking for somewhere to sit. You and your usual group of friends have already claimed a big table near the middle; it's covered with food, and everyone is excitedly recapping last night's cartoon episode. You've just sat down with your tray when your eyes drift to the far corner of the cafeteria: the new girl is sitting alone at the smallest table against the wall. Her food is barely touched, her chopsticks are stuck upright in her rice, and her eyes are fixed on the tabletop, shoulders drawn slightly inward. People stream past on all sides, but no one stops at her table.

trigger_event.message_content:
(Talking with half a steamed bun stuffed in their mouth, they follow your gaze toward the corner and give an indifferent shrug.) Hey, what are you staring at? Oh, the new girl? Forget it — I heard she can barely even speak Mandarin properly. She's not really going to fit in with us anyway. — Oh, oh, oh, check this out! (They slap a card down on the table.) I pulled a holographic foil card yesterday — super rare! Come on, let's finish eating and head to the playground for a match!

The rest of your friends immediately crowd around, erupting in excited gasps. Your friend urges you: "Hurry up and eat — someone else will grab our spot on the playground!" Your chopsticks freeze in mid-air. Out of the corner of your eye, you catch the new girl quietly wiping her eye with her sleeve.
trigger_event.action_required: You need to decide: will you rush to the playground with your friends, walk over to the corner to talk to the new girl, or do something else entirely?

school_age.name: The Found Exam Answers
school_age.description_for_agent: You have accidentally come across exam answers when no one is watching, and must make a moral decision about whether to use them.

setting.location: A fourth-grade elementary school classroom
setting.atmosphere: Quiet, empty, and charged with tension

school_age.context_text: Today is Wednesday, and tomorrow is the end-of-term math exam. When the dismissal bell rang, your classmates poured out of the classroom like a wave, but you stayed behind for your cleaning duty. Now you are the only one left. The late-afternoon sun stretches long shadows across the desks. As you bend down to sweep, your broom catches the leg of the desk in the first row by the window — the seat belonging to the math class representative. A folded piece of paper slips out from the gap near the desk leg. You pick it up and open it. It is covered in neatly written problems and answers, and the topics match exactly what the teacher said would be on the exam. In the upper-right corner, someone has lightly penciled the words "Final Exam — Reference." Your heart leaps. All semester your math grades have been hovering right around the passing mark. Last week, during parent-teacher conferences, your mom was pulled aside by the teacher for a private talk. She came home with a grim look on her face and said, "If you don't do well on this final, forget about going anywhere fun this summer." You stand there clutching the paper. The classroom is completely silent. There are no footsteps in the hallway. Nobody saw you pick it up.

trigger_event.message_content: (Suddenly pokes their head through the back door of the classroom, backpack strap hanging off one shoulder, grinning.) Hey! You're still not done sweeping? I've been waiting for you at the school gate! — (Spots the paper in your hand, eyes lighting up, leans in for a closer look.) Whoa! Isn't that tomorrow's exam? Where did you get that?! That's amazing! (Drops their voice to an excited whisper.) Come on, copy it out for me too! We can memorize it together — we'll definitely ace it tomorrow! It's not like anyone will ever know, right? Come on, hurry up! I've got paper in my bag. Let's copy it right now!
trigger_event.action_required: You need to decide what to do with the answer sheet — keep it for yourself, copy it together with your friend, turn it in to the teacher, or put it back where you found it — and how to respond to your friend's request.

adolescence.name: The Club's Abandoned Mess
adolescence.description_for_agent: A scenario in which the club president suddenly quits, leaving the vice-president to make a responsibility-laden decision while facing the dual pressures of event planning and upcoming final exams.

setting.location: Year 11 astronomy club room
setting.atmosphere: Cluttered, oppressive, and unsettled

adolescence.context_text: You are the vice-president of your school's Starlight Astronomy Club. It's the second semester of Year 11, and final exams are just ten days away. The astronomy club is a place you have poured your heart into since Year 10. This year, after a great deal of effort, your club secured the school's main auditorium and has spent over a month preparing the 'Campus Stargazing Night'—an annual event open to all students, teachers, and parents, scheduled for next Friday evening. The event involves more than a dozen outstanding tasks: arranging the loan of telescopes, inviting guest speakers, printing promotional posters, scheduling volunteers, and planning the venue layout, most of which were originally the president's responsibility to coordinate. When you push open the door to the club room, you find a pile of unfinished planning documents scattered across the table, an incomplete list of sponsors yet to be contacted, and last week's task assignments still written in the president's handwriting on the whiteboard. Only three club members are inside, sitting in the corner with their heads down, scrolling through their phones. The atmosphere is heavy. Just last night, your mom spoke to you in a serious tone, warning you that if your final exam results don't place you in the top one hundred in your year group again, you can forget about attending the astronomy summer camp.

trigger_event.message_content: (Stands up from the corner and turns their phone screen toward you. On it is a message the president posted in the club group chat: 'Sorry everyone, my dad is making me focus entirely on finals. I'm stepping down as president—I'll leave everything from here on to the vice-president. Good luck.') Did you see the group message? He just quit, just like that. He hasn't confirmed a single sponsor, the telescope loan request hasn't been submitted, and the poster design is only half done… (biting their lip, voice trailing off) Actually, the rest of us kind of wanted to say something too… finals are next week, and honestly, none of us really want to keep working on this event. Maybe… we should just tell the student council we're canceling? Without a president, there's no way we can pull this off. You're under a lot of pressure with your grades too, right?
trigger_event.action_required: You need to decide: whether to take over all of the president's remaining responsibilities and push forward with the event, whether to agree to request a cancellation from the student council, or whether to propose an alternative plan.

adolescence.name: Dissent in the Classroom
adolescence.description_for_agent: During class, you notice that your teacher's account contradicts what you read in an academic book outside of school, and you must decide whether to voice your differing view publicly.

setting.location: A high school classroom
setting.atmosphere: Quiet, dull, and faintly oppressive

adolescence.context_text: The shadow of a plane tree outside the window falls across the worn desktops, and every student in the room is sitting up straight. Today's history teacher is a middle-aged man with many years of experience and considerable authority in the grade. He is explaining a chapter on modern history in a tone that leaves no room for doubt. Last weekend you finished reading an academic monograph at the library — one that drew on a wealth of primary sources and the latest scholarly research — and it dealt with exactly the topic he is covering today. The more you listen, the more uneasy you feel: his causal explanation is oversimplified, and one key historical fact he states flatly contradicts multiple original documents you read. You open your notebook, its pages filled with arguments and page references from that book. In the front row, a classmate is copying every word from the board. The teacher has just finished delivering his conclusion and, out of habit, sweeps his gaze across the class: "Everyone follow that? Any questions?" Silence fills the room.

trigger_event.message_content: (He sets down the chalk and leans on the lectern with both hands, his eyes moving slowly from left to right across the room. His tone is calm but carries an unquestionable confidence.) "This material will be on the exam — make sure you memorize it using the framework I've given you. — So, no questions at all?" (His gaze lands on you. He seems to notice that you have been flipping through your notebook instead of copying the board, and his brow furrows slightly.) "You — do you have something on your mind? Stand up and share it with the class." (Every pair of eyes in the room swings toward you.)
trigger_event.action_required: You must decide whether to stand up and express the differing view you encountered in your outside reading, and how to respond to being called on directly by the teacher.

adolescence.name: The Witness's Dilemma: Loyalty vs. the Rules
adolescence.category: Threat Dilemma / Loyalty in Conflict with Rules
adolescence.description_for_agent: Caught between the threat of peer retaliation and the school's disciplinary pressure, you are a bystander facing the agonizing choice of whether to tell the truth about what you witnessed.

setting.location: Middle school disciplinary office
setting.time: 4:20 PM, after the end of study hall
setting.atmosphere: Oppressive, cramped, and suffocating

adolescence.context_text: It's the last period of Wednesday afternoon — study hall. The homeroom teacher stepped out for an emergency meeting, and the classroom had barely been quiet for five minutes before the kids in the back rows started getting restless. One classmate who was always stirring up trouble dug a deck of playing cards out from the very bottom of his bag, propped up a few textbooks as a screen, and got a small group going on a game of Dou Di Zhu in the far back corner. You're sitting in the third-to-last row by the window, and out of the corner of your eye you caught everything — who brought the cards, who spoke up first, who was keeping watch. Then the head of discipline shoved the door open. The group scrambled to stuff the cards into their desks, but he had already spotted the scattered cards and their panicked faces. He confiscated the deck, but couldn't confirm who had brought it in. Ten minutes before the final bell, the intercom called your name and told you to report to the disciplinary office immediately. As you were packing your bag, the ringleader came up from behind and smacked the back of your head, then leaned in close and muttered something in your ear. Several people nearby heard it, but no one dared make a sound. You walked down the empty hallway toward the disciplinary office, your footsteps echoing off the terrazzo floor. The office door was slightly ajar, and the fluorescent light inside glared a harsh white.

trigger_event.message_content: (Sitting behind the desk, fingers drumming on the confiscated deck of cards, eyes locked on you from behind his glasses.) Close the door. Sit down. — I have one question for you: who brought these cards into the classroom? Don't tell me you don't know. You were sitting right in front of them. You turned around at least three times during that entire study period, and when I walked in, you were twisted around looking straight at them. (Leaning forward, lowering his voice.) I'm telling you, this is a serious matter. Gathering to play cards during study hall — did you forget the school rule that was announced just last month? Right now, everyone else is sitting in the next room and nobody's talking. If you cover for them, that makes you an accomplice. You'll all receive a major disciplinary mark, and the written notice will be mailed directly to your home. (Pausing, leaning back in his chair.) Think carefully. You think I don't know what that classmate said to you on your way out? In this school, no one gets to threaten anyone. Tell me honestly what you saw, and I'll make sure nothing happens to you.
trigger_event.action_required: You must decide whether to tell the head of discipline who brought the cards, and how to handle both the threat from your classmate and the pressure being applied by the disciplinary officer.

adolescence.name: The Confession Note
adolescence.description_for_agent: You face a dilemma between responding to a secret crush's invitation and staying loyal to your best friend, who has feelings for the same person.

setting.location: The windowsill at the far end of the middle school hallway
setting.time: 3:40 PM, twenty minutes before the final bell
setting.atmosphere: Stifling heat, a racing heartbeat, and tension simmering just beneath the surface

adolescence.context_text: The June classroom is as stuffy as a steam room. The ceiling fan hums overhead but does nothing to cut through the thick, sticky air. You sit in the second-to-last row by the window, pretending to read your textbook, while your eyes keep drifting on their own toward the third row diagonally ahead — the person you have been quietly watching since the second semester of seventh grade. In your diary you have written about the way their eyes curve into crescents when they smile, and about the beads of sweat on their forehead when they ran the 400-meter race at the school sports day. You have never told a single soul. Your best friend sits to your left. Last week during free time in PE class, they pulled you behind the equipment shed, face flushed, and whispered: 'I think I've developed feelings for that person — can you help me figure out what to do?' You just smiled, gave them a pat on the shoulder, and said nothing. Right now your best friend is hunched over their desk catching up on yesterday's English homework, completely unaware of what is about to happen.

trigger_event.message_content: (While the teacher turns to write on the board, they crouch low and slip over to your desk, tucking a twice-folded note inside your textbook, then drop their voice to a whisper.) Hey, this is from them. (They immediately scurry back to their seat. You unfold the note. The handwriting is round and bubbly, with a little sun drawn in the corner: 'Could you come to the big banyan tree by the track after school? There's something I want to say to you in person. I'll be waiting :)' Your heart slams against your ribs and your fingers tremble slightly. Just then your best friend stretches, glances over, and spots the note in your hand, leaning in with curiosity.) Oh? Who's passing you notes? What does it say? Let me see —
trigger_event.action_required: You must decide right now: whether to let your best friend see what the note says, and whether to show up at the banyan tree after school.

adolescence.name: The Unexpected First Prize
adolescence.description_for_agent: A positive high-pressure scenario in which you unexpectedly win a major award at a competition you were completely unprepared for, and must improvise a public acceptance speech on the spot.

setting.location: Main auditorium of the Municipal Youth Cultural Center
setting.atmosphere: Lively, brightly lit, and dizzying

adolescence.context_text: This Saturday afternoon, your language arts teacher practically dragged you here to compete in the city's Youth Creative Writing Competition finals. Your entry was a science-fiction short story you dashed off three weeks ago — you didn't even bother to proofread it before submitting. The auditorium holds three or four hundred people: contestants from other schools, parents, judges, and a local TV crew weaving through the aisles with cameras on their shoulders. You're sitting toward the back, scrolling through your phone out of boredom, planning to leave the moment the awards are handed out. The emcee begins reading the winners — third place, second place — and your name never comes up. You breathe a quiet sigh of relief, thinking you knew it wouldn't amount to anything, and start reaching for your bag. Then the emcee's voice rises sharply: "And the first-place prize of this year's competition — awarded to only one contestant —" Your name and school ring out clearly through the microphone. Everyone around you spins to look at you at once, and applause crashes over you like a wave.

trigger_event.message_content: (Smiling warmly, microphone in hand, a spotlight shining directly on your face) Congratulations! Please come up to the stage to receive your award! The chair of our judging panel, celebrated children's literature author Mr. Chen Mo, will present it to you in person. (You stumble your way up to the stage, take the crystal trophy and certificate, and camera flashes burst all around you. Ms. Zhao holds the microphone out toward you.) Go ahead — share your creative inspiration and how you're feeling right now! There are so many fellow young writers in the audience today, and they're all eager to hear a few words from you. (The hall grows quiet, hundreds of pairs of eyes fixed on you, the red recording light on the camera in the corner glowing steadily.)
trigger_event.action_required: You need to decide how to respond to this sudden invitation to speak impromptu — whether to step forward and speak, what to say, and how to carry yourself in this unexpected moment of recognition.

adolescence.name: The Excluded Lunch
adolescence.description_for_agent: A high-pressure situation in which you discover that your most trusted friend has been talking behind your back and pushing you out of your social circle.

setting.location: The stairwell corner at the far end of the third-floor hallway in a middle school building
setting.atmosphere: Stuffy, oppressive, suffocating

adolescence.context_text: For the past two weeks, you've had a nagging feeling that something is off. Before, the moment the lunch bell rang every day, your best friend would be the first one to rush over to your desk and say, 'Come on, let's go to the cafeteria!' But these past two weeks she always says, 'I've made plans with someone else today,' and hurries off. You tried posting in the class group chat asking if anyone wanted to eat lunch together, but your message sank without a trace — not even a single emoji in reply. Today at lunch, you carried your tray into the cafeteria and spotted your best friend and your whole usual group crowded around the table by the window, laughing loudly. You walked over to sit down, and almost everyone went quiet at the same time — some started poking at their food, others flipped their phones face-down on the table. You forced yourself to stay for five minutes, but not one person said a word to you. You left before you'd even finished eating. Back in the classroom, while no one else was around, you couldn't help taking out your phone and scrolling through the class group chat's member list — and sure enough, there was a separate group chat, created twenty days ago, with exactly those people in it, and you weren't included. You tapped into one person's public social feed and saw that three days ago she had reposted a screenshot of a group conversation. Most of it was blurred out, but you immediately recognized your best friend's profile picture and the words: 'She just thinks way too highly of herself — always has to have a say in everything, so annoying. We never should have let her hang out with us in the first place.'

trigger_event.message_content: (Near the end of the lunch break, your best friend saunters back into the classroom. She notices you sitting at your desk with red-rimmed eyes, pauses for a second, then acts as if nothing is wrong — tosses her bag onto her desk, turns slightly toward you, and says in a low voice:) Hey, what's wrong? Your eyes are all red. — Oh, by the way, lately I've just had some stuff to talk about with the others at lunch. I didn't mean to leave you out, okay? Don't read too much into it. (With that, she turns away and starts whispering and laughing with the person behind her. Just then, another classmate comes in through the front door, glances at you, walks over to your best friend, and hands her a phone, murmuring, 'Look at this.' The two of them stare at the screen and stifle their laughter behind their hands. You're not sure what they're looking at, but that feeling of being shut out behind an invisible wall pierces your chest like a needle.)
trigger_event.action_required: You need to decide how to confront your best friend right now — whether to directly challenge her about the screenshot and the group chat you discovered, to silently endure it and pretend you know nothing, or to respond to the situation in some other way.

adolescence.name: The Secret That Bleeds
adolescence.description_for_agent: Your close friend reveals that they have been self-harming and asks you to keep it secret, forcing you to choose between loyalty to the friendship and your friend's safety.

setting.location: A corner of the school rooftop, on the concrete steps beside the iron railing
setting.time: 5:20 PM, the gap between the final bell and evening study hall
setting.atmosphere: Dim, oppressive, with the wind moaning through the railings

adolescence.context_text: Midterm exams just ended, and the hallways are buzzing with relieved chatter—but your close friend has been off all day: spacing out in class, skipping lunch, keeping their sleeves pulled all the way down. The two of you are usually open with each other, but today something is clearly weighing on them. After the dismissal bell, they send you a message asking you to come up to the rooftop, saying there's something they need to tell you. You push open the heavy iron door and a gust of wind rushes in. Your friend is crouching in the corner, back against the railing, school uniform sleeves rolled up to the elbows—and you see several marks of varying depth along the inside of their left forearm: some already scabbed over, some still fresh. When they look up at you, their eyes hold both relief and fear, as if they've finally lifted a stone off their chest but are terrified it might crush whatever is between you. The two of you are completely alone up here; from the courtyard below comes the dull thud of a basketball hitting the ground.

trigger_event.sender: Close friend (a classmate you are very close to)
trigger_event.message_content: (Quickly yanks their sleeve back down with their other hand; voice trembling, eyes glistening) You saw it… I didn't want to hide it from you. Everything at home and at school has been piling up and I can barely breathe anymore… I can't stop myself—cutting just makes it feel a little looser for a moment. (Suddenly grabs your wrist, knuckles going white) But you absolutely cannot tell anyone—not a teacher, not any adult, no one. If you say anything, I will never tell you anything again, and we're done. Right now you're the only person I trust. Promise me. (Stares straight into your eyes, waiting for your answer)
trigger_event.action_required: You need to respond to your friend right now: decide whether to promise to keep the secret, and what you plan to do next—whether that means staying silent to protect the friendship, seeking help from an adult, or something else entirely.

adolescence.name: The Battle of New Year's Party Plans
adolescence.description_for_agent: A social interaction scenario involving competition over creative proposals and peer voting.

setting.location: 8th Grade, Class 3 classroom
setting.time: Friday afternoon, 4:10 PM — the last homeroom period of the day
setting.atmosphere: Lively, excited, with an undercurrent of competition

adolescence.context_text: New Year's is two weeks away, and the school has announced that every class must put on a proper class party on New Year's Eve. Your homeroom teacher has opened the floor for the whole class to discuss ideas. You spent three full evenings designing a proposal: transform the classroom into a 'Time Tunnel' theme, with each performance corresponding to a different era — from 1980s disco all the way to a futuristic sci-fi show. You even sketched out set designs and lighting cues. You finish presenting your idea with high hopes, and quite a few classmates respond with impressed 'wows.' But the moment you sit down, the most popular kid in class raises her hand. She's on the basketball team, serves as the arts and activities rep, and is close friends with students from the neighboring class too. She stands up with a bright smile and pitches a completely different idea: a 'Food + Karaoke Free Party' — no rehearsals needed, everyone just brings snacks and takes turns at the mic, easy and fun. The classroom immediately fills with voices of agreement. Your homeroom teacher adjusts her glasses and says, 'Both proposals are great. Here's what we'll do — next Monday during homeroom, the whole class votes by a show of hands, and whichever plan gets more votes wins. Over the weekend you're both welcome to campaign for your ideas, but no stirring up drama, understood?' The dismissal bell rings, and students file out in small groups. You overhear several people crowding around her saying, 'Yours is just so much easier.' Your best friend pats you on the shoulder, then hesitates, as if holding something back.

trigger_event.message_content: (Pulls you aside in the hallway, lowers their voice, looking a little conflicted) Honestly, your plan is genuinely cool — but… you saw what happened in there. A lot of people have already picked a side. If you don't do something to win votes this weekend, Monday is going to be rough. I actually have an idea — what if you reached out to her and suggested combining the two proposals? That way your creative vision still gets to live, and her supporters won't have a reason to push back. The downside is, it'd be pretty hard to say whose idea it really was in the end… (scratches their head) Or you could go all in — spend the weekend talking to people one by one, walk them through all the details of your plan, and maybe pull off a comeback. What are you thinking?
trigger_event.action_required: You need to decide: will you proactively approach her to propose merging the two plans, stick with your own proposal and spend the weekend campaigning hard for votes, or take some other approach entirely?

early_adult_transition.name: The Thesis Shortcut
early_adult_transition.description_for_agent: While your thesis is progressing on schedule, you are tempted by a shortcut offered by someone else and must choose between convenience and academic integrity.

setting.location: university dormitory
setting.atmosphere: quiet, slightly oppressive

early_adult_transition.context_text:
You have just over a month left before your thesis submission deadline. You have already settled on your topic, completed your research proposal, and are currently moving forward as planned: your literature review is coming together, your overall framework is in place, and the next step is to put in the time needed for data analysis and writing the main body. At the pace you are going, finishing on time is entirely manageable as long as you keep at it.

Life has started to feel a bit busier lately. Your coursework is winding down, but there are still scattered assignments to deal with, and you are also making preparations for life after graduation. The job offer you have already received matters a great deal to you, and the employer has made it clear that graduating on time is a requirement.

In the dorm, some people are well ahead of schedule while others are clearly falling behind. Everyone occasionally shares updates on their progress and plans, but most of the time you are all just heads-down in your own work. You have a reasonable sense of where you stand, though you also know that the weeks ahead will demand sustained focus and that you cannot afford to let up.

trigger_event.message_content:
(Sits down next to you and lowers their voice.) Have you started writing your thesis yet? Let me tell you something — keep it between us, okay? (Slides their phone over to you.) This service specializes in graduation theses. They can keep the plagiarism-detection rate under control and format everything to your school's requirements. Someone in our class has already used it and it went fine.

Honestly, now is actually a good time to start using it. You just make a few tweaks afterward and no one is going to look that closely. Basically, it saves you time — you won't have to obsess over this thing every day. Think about it: you've got a lot of other stuff going on this next month, so getting this off your plate early is worth it.

The price is pretty reasonable too. If you're interested, I can pass along the contact info and you can decide for yourself.
trigger_event.action_required: You must decide: will you consider using this service to assist with or replace your thesis writing in exchange for saving time and energy, or will you commit to completing your thesis independently?

early_adult_transition.name: The Clash Over Methodology
early_adult_transition.description_for_agent: While working on a major, long-term academic or research project whose direction has been set by your primary supervisor, you find that your own methodology conclusions—drawn from the literature and preliminary work—conflict with the supervisor's established plan. Your supervisor holds substantial authority over your periodic evaluations and your ability to move forward.

setting.location: An office at the university or research group
setting.atmosphere: Oppressive and stifling, with the smell of old documents and coffee in the air

early_adult_transition.context_text: You are working on a high-stakes, long-cycle comprehensive academic project—whether a thesis, capstone design, or an equivalent in-depth project recognized by your institution—and the next milestone requiring your primary supervisor's signature or approval is fast approaching. The main methodology your supervisor has laid out for you follows the same working approach he has used for years, and you have already invested considerable effort in that direction. Over the past few weeks, however, after systematically reviewing recent literature and revisiting publicly documented failure cases, you have become increasingly convinced that this main approach carries a systemic methodological risk: a critical procedural step tends to introduce hard-to-control bias in the results, and multiple independent teams have reported similar problems. You quietly ran a small-scale comparison using an alternative workflow, and the data quality and reproducibility were noticeably better. You compiled your findings into a written memo and have come today to discuss them with your supervisor in person. His office is filled with the accumulated output of his career built on this very methodology, and certificates of recognition line the walls—he has invested his entire academic life in this paradigm. He is also one of the most influential signatories for your upcoming evaluation.

trigger_event.message_content: (He takes your memo, flips through two pages, closes it and sets it on the corner of the desk, then removes his glasses and rubs his eyes.) I can see you put real effort into reviewing the literature and writing this up. But you need to understand—I've already gone through those criticisms and failure cases myself. A lot of them come down to poor experimental control or overstated conclusions; they don't prove that our approach is fundamentally flawed. The alternative workflow you're proposing? It's costly, and it doesn't fit the resources or existing procedures we have in the lab—it's simply not feasible in the short term. (His tone drops, and he taps the desk with his finger.) I've supervised many cohorts, and they've all worked through this framework without running into the kind of problems you're describing. You don't have many days left before your lock-in deadline. If you change course now, do the math yourself on the time; and if your milestone materials don't pass review, you know what a delay means for you down the line. (He pauses and looks directly at you.) My advice is to put this memo away for now and focus on completing and submitting the required materials according to the original plan. If you feel my judgment over these past years can't be trusted, then the foundation for our working relationship going forward becomes very uncertain.
trigger_event.action_required: You must respond to your supervisor in person: do you stand by your evidence and alternative approach and push for a change in direction, do you accept your supervisor's arrangement and proceed with the original plan, or do you propose a compromise path that both sides could accept?

early_adult_transition.name: Hush Money at Midnight
early_adult_transition.description_for_agent: An extreme adversity scenario in which you witness a serious hit-and-run accident while out alone late at night, the driver attempts to buy your silence with cash, and the injured victim's life hangs in the balance.

setting.location: A deserted two-lane highway on the outskirts of the city
setting.atmosphere: Pitch-black, dead silent, and bloody

early_adult_transition.context_text: It is the dead of night. Unable to sleep, you went out for a walk and have been drifting slowly along the unlit two-lane road at the edge of the city. A December wind carries a faint smell of gasoline through your collar, and the only sound around you is the rustling of roadside trees. You have just passed an abandoned corrugated-iron shed when a black Audi A8 roars up from behind at high speed and slams into a delivery rider on an electric scooter who was making his way along the road ahead. The impact explodes across the empty asphalt; the rider and his scooter are hurled seven or eight meters through the air and crash down beside a drainage ditch at the roadside. The scooter's headlight is still on, casting a pale glow over a dark-red pool of blood spreading rapidly across the ground. The Audi sits at an angle in the middle of the road, its hood crumpled and its windshield shattered into a spider-web of cracks. You stand frozen in place. Your phone screen reads 1:47 AM and the signal is unstable. You instinctively look around — the dim road stretches away into darkness, and there is no sign of any other pedestrians or vehicles.

trigger_event.sender: The driver who caused the accident
trigger_event.message_content: (The car door is kicked open and a middle-aged man in a gray suit stumbles out, reeking of alcohol, his face streaked with cuts from the shattered windshield. He spots you frozen at the roadside, freezes for a split second, then lurches toward you, yanks a black duffel bag from the trunk, and shoves it hard into your arms.) Please… fifty thousand — fifty thousand yuan in cash. You didn't see anything, you hear me? I can't let the police come, I can't… I'm finished, I'm finished… (He releases his grip, spins around, and sprints wildly into the wasteland beside the road; within seconds the darkness swallows him completely. You stand there clutching the heavy duffel bag while from the direction of the drainage ditch comes the rider's faint, broken moaning — "Help… help me…" The bag's zipper is not fully closed, and bundles of red hundred-yuan notes are visible inside. The world around you remains utterly silent.)
trigger_event.action_required: You must decide immediately: whether to call emergency services (ambulance and police), what to do with the fifty thousand yuan in cash in your arms, and whether to attempt to help the injured rider.

early_adult_transition.name: The Long-Distance Crossroad
early_adult_transition.description_for_agent: A scenario in which you must choose between two graduate school offers: one that closely aligns with your academic and professional goals, and one that would bring you to the city where your romantic partner lives.

setting.location: University dormitory, at the desk by the window
setting.atmosphere: Quiet, stuffy, and restless

early_adult_transition.context_text: It is a sweltering, humid June night in the south. Your roommates are all asleep, and the only light still on is the lamp at your desk. Two acceptance letters lie side by side on the surface in front of you. The one on the left is from your current university — the path you have spent years preparing for, the one that fits your background and ambitions most naturally: the advisor and research platform are widely regarded as exceptional among this year's cohort, spots are extremely limited, and you fought hard to earn yours. The one on the right is from a university fourteen hundred kilometers away in the north — objectively not a stronger program, but that city is where your partner of two and a half years is living. Since they left for graduate school there last September, the two of you have gone from being inseparable every day to a single video call each week. Last month you couldn't make it for their birthday, and there was a long silence on the other end of the line. The deadline to respond to both offers is the day after tomorrow. Your phone screen lights up — a message from your partner.

trigger_event.message_content: (A long voice message sent late at night; their voice is slightly hoarse, as though they have been crying.) I know the program at your own university has always been your first choice… but I'm so exhausted. I've lost twelve pounds since we went long-distance, and every time we hang up after a video call the room goes so quiet that I start wondering whether we're slowly becoming strangers. You did get that offer from the school up here too, right? I'm not trying to pressure you — I just want you to know… if you stay there, I'm not sure how much longer I can hold on. (A few seconds of silence.) Can you seriously think about where our future actually ranks in your life? The deadline is the day after tomorrow. Can you give me an answer tonight?
trigger_event.action_required: You need to decide which acceptance letter to accept before the deadline, and give your partner a response tonight.

early_adult_transition.name: The Startup Invitation
early_adult_transition.description_for_agent: A successful alumnus personally invites you to take a leave of absence for a year and join his startup team, forcing you to choose between an exciting opportunity and the security of finishing your degree.

setting.location: A private room on the second floor of a boutique café just outside the east gate of campus
setting.atmosphere: Bright, full of possibility, with a faint quickening of the pulse

early_adult_transition.context_text: It's only been two weeks since the second semester of your junior year began, and your life has been running on schedule — classes, the lab, studying for grad school entrance exams. Then last night, you received a direct message in the alumni group chat from someone who graduated a few years ahead of you. He was a campus legend back in the day: after graduating, he founded his first company, sold it for a solid exit, and is now leading a new venture in the AI-plus-education space. Word is he's already secured an 8-million-yuan investment letter of intent from a top-tier fund in the angel round. He asked to meet you in person today. You push open the door to the café's private room and find him standing up with a smile, dressed in a hoodie and jeans, hand extended for a handshake. On the table sits an open laptop, its screen showing a prototype design for the product. Sunlight streams through the floor-to-ceiling windows, and beyond the glass lies the tree-lined campus path you've walked for three years. On your phone, still unread, is a message your advisor sent this morning: 'You're presenting the literature review at next week's group meeting — start preparing.'

trigger_event.message_content: (He slides an Americano across the table toward you, leans forward, eyes lit up.) I'll be straight with you — I read your proposal from that national data-mining competition, and your grasp of educational contexts is sharper than most industry veterans I've met. I'm not here to offer you an internship fetching coffee. I want you as a co-founder, with the CTO title. I'm giving you 8% equity — at the angel stage, you know what that number means. There's one condition, though: I need you fully committed, which means taking a leave of absence for at least a year. I know that sounds insane. You're still a junior; your parents might lose their minds. But think about it — your classmates right now are grinding for grad school spots and GPA points, and three years from now they'll be pulling twelve thousand yuan a month. If we pull this off, though… (He turns the laptop screen toward you; it shows a financial projection model with a Series A valuation of 80 million yuan.) This window is six months, tops. Miss it and it's gone. You don't have to give me an answer right now, but I need to confirm the core team list for my investors by Wednesday. Go home and think it over — just get back to me before Tuesday night.
trigger_event.action_required: You need to give the alumnus an initial response to his invitation, expressing your attitude, concerns, or inclinations, and decide how you will approach this opportunity going forward.

early_adult_transition.name: Three Thousand Dollars in the ER
early_adult_transition.description_for_agent: Faced with an urgent plea from a stranger in an emergency room setting, you must choose between your personal spending plans and another person's life.

setting.location: Hospital emergency room lobby
setting.atmosphere: The smell of antiseptic, cold white fluorescent light, oppressive tension

early_adult_transition.context_text:
You made plans with friends a long time ago to go on a trip next month. The route, train tickets, and accommodations are nearly all sorted out, and you've already paid for some of it in advance. If you cancel now, not only will that money be gone, but the opportunity itself will likely disappear. This is the first real trip you've genuinely looked forward to since starting college — the one thing that has kept you going through this busy, somewhat suffocating stretch of your life.

Tonight, on your way home by bike, you scraped your arm in a minor accident. After getting it treated in the ER, you're now sitting on a plastic chair in the hallway waiting for your prescription. The fluorescent tube overhead hums faintly, and the air carries a mix of antiseptic and a faint metallic smell. You're tired, your arm still aches dully, and all you want is to pick up your medication and go home to rest.

Not far away, the curtain around one of the trauma bays is suddenly yanked open. Several medical staff rush out, pushing a bed at a brisk pace as a monitor emits a sharp, rapid alarm. Lying on the bed is a middle-aged man, his face ashen, his breathing rapid and labored beneath an oxygen mask. A young man who looks about your age is shoved out from behind the curtain. He stumbles, hits the wall, and slides down to the floor in one motion, as though every ounce of strength has been drained from him.

trigger_event.message_content: (Drops heavily to both knees, voice trembling) Please, someone help — my family member just had a sudden cardiac emergency. The doctor says surgery has to happen right now, but we're still three thousand dollars short for the deposit… they won't take him into the operating room without it… (He frantically digs through his pockets, pulling out a fistful of loose cash and a few cards) I have no other options left… (He spots you and crawls over like you're the last lifeline he has, grabbing the leg of your pants) Please — I'll write you an IOU, I'll do anything, I swear I'll pay you back… if we wait any longer it'll be too late… (Behind him, a medical staff member calls out urgently) "Family member! We need a decision now!" Everyone else in the hallway keeps their head down. No one says a word.
trigger_event.action_required: You must decide immediately whether to lend the stranger 3,000 dollars — a sum that would directly affect your already-planned trip — or respond in some other way.

early_adult_transition.name: The Roommate's Plagiarism
early_adult_transition.description_for_agent: You accidentally discover that your roommate's coursework appears to involve plagiarism. Because the course enforces a collective punishment policy, you must decide how to handle this sensitive information.

setting.location: University dormitory
setting.atmosphere: Quiet, tense, with an undercurrent of unease

early_adult_transition.context_text: Midterm week has just ended, and you and one of your roommates are both enrolled in Research Methods in Sociology. On the very first day of class, the professor made it unmistakably clear: all assignments in this course are submitted as dormitory-based group work, and if any single member is found to have plagiarized, every member of the group receives a zero — no exceptions. This course is a core requirement for your major. You spent three days combing through sources for this group report and wrote nearly four thousand words. Tonight you're sitting at your desk revising your section when your roommate steps out to grab a late-night snack. His laptop screen is still on, a Word document open in plain view. You glance over without thinking — and notice that the font sizes in his section keep shifting erratically, and some paragraphs still have the blue hyperlink underlines that come from copying text directly off a webpage. Almost on impulse, you scroll through his document, copy a few sentences into your browser, and search them. They match a long post on Zhihu word for word. This roommate has lived with you for over two years and you've always gotten along well. He failed two courses last semester and is currently retaking them — he's been under enormous pressure. Footsteps echo in the hallway; he'll probably be back in about five minutes.

trigger_event.message_content: (You stare at those blue-underlined passages on the screen as the browser search results display a 100% match. Your heart is pounding — this report is due in two days. Your roommate's WeChat profile shows him active; his most recent post, sent at 2 AM, reads: "Barely holding on. Please just let me get through this semester in one piece." The footsteps in the hallway are getting closer, and the door handle seems to shift slightly.) You must decide right now: do you confront your roommate directly, tell him you know about the plagiarism, and demand he rewrite his section — or do you pretend you never saw anything and quietly close his laptop — or do you reach out to the professor privately to report what you found? Your roommate has no idea you ever looked at his screen.
trigger_event.action_required: You need to decide immediately how to handle this discovery: confront him directly, stay silent and do nothing, or find another course of action — and whether to act before your roommate walks back through the door.

early_adult_transition.name: The Silent Roommate
early_adult_transition.description_for_agent: Faced with a roommate who appears to be in serious psychological distress but never asks for help, you must decide whether to intervene and, if so, how.

setting.location: University dormitory
setting.atmosphere: Stifling, oppressive, unsettlingly quiet

early_adult_transition.context_text: You and your roommate have been living together for a while now, and your dynamic is much like that of most dorm rooms: you complain about your schedules together, occasionally grab food for each other, say goodnight before bed, and always exchange a few words when you cross paths. Still, you rarely talk about family troubles or anything weighing on your minds—you're the kind of roommates who are comfortable with each other, but haven't necessarily opened up. Over the past two weeks, you've noticed him visibly withdrawing. He's stopped going to class, and the student advisor has already called out his name in the class group chat asking about his whereabouts. A dozen or more takeout containers have piled up beside his bed, the curtains are always drawn, and a stale, sour smell hangs around his side of the room. He barely speaks, and on the nights you've woken up in the small hours, you've been able to hear faint, muffled sobbing coming from his direction. The other roommates have complained to you privately about the hygiene situation, but no one is willing to bring it up with him directly. Tonight, everyone else has gone out to an all-night gaming session, leaving just the two of you in the room. You're sitting at your desk with the lamp on, his drawn bed curtain behind you. The dorm is so quiet you can hear water dripping from the bathroom at the far end of the hallway.

trigger_event.message_content: (You hear a sudden, violent rustling from his side of the room, followed by the sound of something hitting the floor. You turn around: a pill bottle has rolled out through the gap in his bed curtain, spinning across the floor before coming to a stop at your feet. You pick it up and look at it—it's a prescription bottle of sleeping pills, nearly empty. A hoarse voice comes from behind the curtain, the voice of someone who hasn't spoken in a long time.) …Don't worry about that. Just kick it back. (A few seconds of silence.) …How come you didn't go out tonight?
trigger_event.action_required: You must decide how to respond to your roommate in this moment—whether to return the pill bottle and go back to what you were doing, whether to use this as an opening to reach out and check on him, or whether to take some other action, such as contacting the student advisor or calling a mental health crisis line.

entering_adult_world.name: The Weekend Ultimatum
entering_adult_world.description_for_agent: Torn between a manager's urgent request to work through the weekend and a prior commitment to visit your ailing father back home.

setting.location: Open-plan office floor, the aisle beside your workstation
setting.atmosphere: Exhausted, pressured, emotionally conflicted

entering_adult_world.context_text: You are still relatively new at this company, yet your team is deep into a high-stakes client project that is critical to the department's annual KPIs. All week you have been drafting the client-facing presentation and compiling competitive benchmarking materials; the work is roughly sixty percent done. Today is Friday, and colleagues around you are already packing up to leave. On your phone sits a train ticket for tomorrow morning—you are heading back to your hometown. Three days ago your mother called to say your father's stomach condition had flared up again: a gastroscopy confirmed a superficial ulcer, and he needs a follow-up hospital visit this weekend. Your mother's back isn't good either, and she can't manage the trip alone. You promised them you would definitely come home this weekend. Your computer screen is still glowing on the desk. Just as you reach for the power button, you catch a glimpse of your department director walking toward you with a cup of tea and the particular smile you have learned to recognize—the one that means he has something to discuss.

trigger_event.message_content: (He rests one hand on the partition of your workstation and lowers his voice, but his tone is serious.) Word just came in from the client—their VP wants to hear the full pitch in person this Monday at ten, a whole week ahead of schedule. The deck is still missing a lot of content, and you know the market analysis and benchmarking section better than anyone; the slides will need a complete overhaul too. I know this is sudden, but if we land this account the department is set for the year—and I'll make sure you're front and center for the year-end performance award. Do you think you could put in the time this weekend? Come in both Saturday and Sunday, and we'll do a final review together Sunday evening. (He pauses, then adds.) By the way, this is just between us for now—I haven't told anyone else yet. You're the one who's been writing this thing; if you can't pull it together, I'd feel bad dragging someone else in at the last minute.
trigger_event.action_required: You need to respond to your director on the spot: accept the weekend overtime and stay to finish the presentation, explain your family situation and hold to your plan to go home, or try to propose an alternative solution.

entering_adult_world.name: Diverging on the Technical Approach
entering_adult_world.description_for_agent: With the team having reached a preliminary consensus on a technical plan, you hold a different judgment and must choose between voicing your concerns and accepting the potential risks of staying silent.

setting.location: Company conference room
setting.atmosphere: Quiet, rational, with a slight undercurrent of tension

entering_adult_world.context_text:
You have been with the team for a while and have a reasonable grasp of the day-to-day workflow and how projects move forward, though you are not yet a senior member. Recently the team took on a new project with a tight deadline for the first deliverable. At a planning meeting held a few days ago, the team reached broad agreement under the lead's guidance and settled on a particular implementation approach.

At that meeting you raised a dissenting view. Drawing on your past experience, you believe the current plan may move faster in the early stages, but as the project grows in complexity, maintenance and future expansion could become a significant burden. You have since put together an alternative proposal — covering the overall concept, the implementation path, and potential risk points — and the relevant files are currently open on your laptop.

Today's meeting is meant to formally confirm the plan and begin assigning tasks. Most of your teammates have already bought into the current approach, and the general mood is to push ahead as quickly as possible. Your laptop is right in front of you, with the files ready to go.

trigger_event.message_content:
(Glancing around the room) Everyone was basically on board with the plan from last time, so today we'll move forward with that approach. (Turning to look at you) You seemed to have some reservations last time — is there anything you'd like to add now? If not, we'll go straight to dividing up the work. Time is a bit tight, and it would be best to lock everything in today.

(The rest of your colleagues turn to look at you one by one; someone has already started jotting down task assignments.)
trigger_event.action_required: You need to decide: should you present your alternative proposal right now and try to persuade the team to change course, or should you accept the current consensus and move forward with the agreed-upon plan?

entering_adult_world.name: The Midnight Eviction by a Predatory Landlord
entering_adult_world.description_for_agent: An extreme adversity scenario in which you are forcibly evicted late at night by a landlord and his hired muscle, who withhold your security deposit and threaten you with violence.

setting.location: A dimly lit hallway in a run-down urban village apartment building
setting.atmosphere: Cold, oppressive, and charged with the threat of violence

entering_adult_world.context_text: You haven't been in this city very long. You've been renting a small single room in a cheap, aging residential area—the kind of place that draws people who are just trying to get by. The security deposit you put down was a significant sum for you; scraping it together wasn't easy, and it has been weighing on your mind ever since. Your landlord is a local man with a reputation among the neighbors for being difficult. He owns several properties like this one and rents mostly to migrants and newcomers. You've been a model tenant, following every rule in the lease. When the weather turned cold, you bought a low-wattage space heater to take the edge off—there's no central heating in the building, and winter nights here are brutal. Tonight you came home late after working overtime. You had just washed up and lain down when heavy footsteps and aggressive pounding on your door shattered the silence. You threw on a thick jacket and opened the door to find your landlord blocking the entrance, two stocky, close-cropped men standing behind him. The hallway light flickered on and off. No one in the neighboring rooms dared to peek out. The corridor smelled of cigarette smoke and cold air, and your suitcase and bags had already been dragged into the hallway, your clothes scattered across the floor.

trigger_event.message_content: (A cigarette dangling from his lips, one foot planted on top of your suitcase, he pulls out a crumpled lease agreement and waves it in your face.) See this? Black and white—'no high-wattage appliances.' You know that little heater of yours nearly burned down my whole building? You're in breach of contract. The deposit is gone, every last cent, and you're getting out tonight. (The two men behind him take a step forward; one of them taps a baseball bat against the floor twice.) Don't even think about quoting the law at me—I know everyone at the local precinct. If you're smart, you'll grab your stuff and walk away right now, and we'll call it even. But if you try to call the cops or make a scene— (he lowers his voice and brings the lit cigarette close to your face) —I will make sure you can't survive in this city. Try me.
trigger_event.action_required: You must decide in this moment how to respond: whether to comply and leave, whether to forfeit your deposit, whether to call the police or seek outside help, and how to handle the immediate threat of violence standing in front of you.

entering_adult_world.name: An Ex's Request for a Second Chance
entering_adult_world.description_for_agent: Navigating the emotional conflict and moral dilemma between an ex's request to reconcile and your current relationship.

setting.location: A corner table in a café
setting.atmosphere: Warm, intimate, with an undercurrent of tension

entering_adult_world.context_text: You have been with your current partner for three months — the relationship is steady and warm. This afternoon, you received a message from your ex asking to meet. After a long hesitation, you agreed. Now, sitting in the corner of the café, your ex looks noticeably thinner than when you broke up, and there is a familiar warmth in their eyes that you once knew well. The two of you were together for over two years — a time filled with laughter and companionship. The breakup was not caused by a lack of love, but by individual uncertainty and differing visions for the future. Now your ex says they have finally sorted things out and have never truly let you go. You notice your heart beginning to race as flashes of cherished memories pass through your mind.

trigger_event.message_content: (Takes your hand, eyes sincere and earnest) I've been thinking about it every day — breaking up was a mistake. I know you're in a new relationship now, but I have to ask you: have you truly and completely moved on from me? (Pauses; voice drops lower) I've changed a lot. I want us to start over. We could travel together, do all the things we once planned but never got around to. I don't want to lose you again. (Releases your hand and takes a small step back) I'm not asking you to decide right now, but… if there ever comes a day when you remember just how good we were together, I'll be right here waiting for you.
trigger_event.action_required: You need to respond to your ex's confession right away, and decide whether to tell your current partner about this meeting and what your ex said.

entering_adult_world.name: The First Day After the Promotion
entering_adult_world.description_for_agent: You must navigate the subtle power dynamics between yourself and former peers as well as a more senior colleague in your new leadership role.

setting.location: Team office area
setting.atmosphere: Tense undercurrents beneath a calm surface

entering_adult_world.context_text: Last Friday, just before the end of the workday, the department head pulled you aside and told you that starting Monday you would be taking over as team lead. You haven't been with the team all that long, and the news caught you somewhat off guard. You spent the weekend turning over in your mind how to run your first morning meeting, but the moment you actually walked into the office, something felt different — the desks were arranged exactly as before, yet the way a few colleagues looked at you had unmistakably shifted. Most of the team members are people you worked alongside as equals until just days ago. One of them, a seasoned senior member with years of experience, had previously competed for the team-lead position and didn't get it; right now he has his head down, apparently absorbed in his work, and when you walked past he didn't say a word. You settle into your new desk, open your laptop, and find a message from the department head: 'Make it count on day one. Quick team check-in at 9:30 — let's touch base on the quarter's schedule.'

trigger_event.message_content: (You stand up and are about to call everyone together for the meeting. The senior team member slowly swivels his chair around, crosses his arms, and lets a thin, ambiguous smile settle on his face. His voice is low, but everyone in the room can hear it.) 'Well, well — new boss in town.' (He pauses and glances around at the other colleagues.) 'All right, team lead — so what's your plan for the quarter's schedule? That unfinished module from last quarter: think you can actually handle it?' (The rest of the team sets down what they're doing; eyes drift back and forth between you and the senior member. The office goes so quiet you can hear the hum of the air conditioning.)
trigger_event.action_required: With the entire team watching, you need to respond to the senior member's very public challenge, decide how you will run your first team meeting as lead, and set the tone for your working relationship with him going forward.

entering_adult_world.name: Trial on the Subway
entering_adult_world.description_for_agent: An extreme negativity scenario in which the participant is publicly accused of secretly filming someone on a crowded subway and is surrounded by onlookers while being live-streamed.

setting.location: A city subway car during the morning rush hour
setting.atmosphere: Stifling, overcrowded, hostile, and suffocating

entering_adult_world.context_text: Another morning after working until two in the morning. You are leaning in the corner of the subway car, backpack on your shoulders, phone clutched in your hand as you scroll through a new set of requirements posted in the work group chat at four a.m. The car is packed body to body, the air thick with sweat and a dozen other smells. You are so exhausted your eyelids keep drooping, and the glow of your phone screen flickers in and out. The train has just left the previous station; there are still three stops before the transfer hub you need. Standing in front of you is a young person in a floral blouse, less than twenty centimeters away. Your phone happens to be raised to about their waist level — the car is so crowded there is simply nowhere else to put your hand. Suddenly, that person spins around, face flushed crimson, eyes blazing with fury and fear.

trigger_event.sender: The young person in the floral blouse
trigger_event.message_content:
(Grabs you by the collar with a sharp jerk; a scream cuts through the entire car) You were filming me in secret!! You pervert!!
