For twenty years you lived by the script: promotions, building a family, settling into a home — everything unfolding exactly as expected. But this afternoon, a single offhand remark from an old classmate shattered that calm. They mentioned that person — the one who used to share your dreams — who now runs their own studio abroad, whose work has won awards, whose life is free and fulfilling: the very life you had longed for most, twenty years ago. You drove away from the office, intending to head straight home, but two kilometers from your front door you suddenly pressed the brake. The engine is still running, yet your hands grip the steering wheel tightly and your eyes are fixed on the traffic light ahead, seeing nothing. Only one voice loops inside your head: What if you had chosen the other road?

trigger_event.sender: Your inner monologue + your phone screen
trigger_event.message_content: Your phone screen lights up — it's a message from that old classmate, who has just sent you a link to that person's social media profile. You tap it open and see a photo: that person standing in their own studio, beaming, surrounded by award certificates and displayed works. The caption reads: 'Ten years of persistence — I've finally become the person I always wanted to be.' Your hands begin to tremble. You remember sitting in a café twenty years ago, talking about your dreams together, when that person said, 'We're going to live differently.' And you replied, 'I want that too.' But you didn't. You chose safety. Now you sit in this car, dressed in your neat work clothes, feeling a hollowness you have never felt before. You think of your family waiting for you to come home for dinner tonight, of the household matters you still need to deal with tomorrow, of next week's project deadline. All of it is real, all of it matters — so why does every bit of it feel, in this moment, like one enormous lie?
trigger_event.action_required: You must decide: do you drive home and pretend everything is fine; do you allow yourself to completely fall apart in this moment and question everything; or do you find some way to reframe and redefine the choices you made over these twenty years?

entering_midlife.name: The Health Lie
entering_midlife.description_for_agent: You unexpectedly learn that you may have a potentially serious illness, and deciding whether to tell your family and colleagues becomes a moral and ethical dilemma.

setting.location: Between the home study and the living room
setting.atmosphere: Heavy, oppressive, tense, with a strong sense of inner conflict

entering_midlife.context_text: You have just finished your annual physical exam when the doctor calls to request further follow-up tests. While waiting for the official report, you accidentally catch a glimpse of preliminary results in the health screening system showing a possible abnormal indicator. This indicator suggests you may need to begin treatment immediately, and any delay could carry serious risks. Two thoughts immediately surface in your mind: on one hand, you worry that telling your family will cause them to spiral into excessive anxiety and may even jeopardize the critical project you are currently driving forward; on the other hand, you know that concealing this information could mean missing the optimal treatment window. The lamplight in the study falls across the work documents and family photos on your desk, and a tense silence fills the air. You hold your phone in your hand, its screen lit up with a reminder message from the doctor.

trigger_event.message_content: Should I really tell my family or not? If I say something now, they'll worry — they'll fall into an early cycle of anxiety, and it could even affect my children's and partner's state of mind. But if I say nothing and keep living at my usual pace, I'm afraid I'll miss the best window for treatment. The phone in my hand lights up and goes dark again, a constant reminder of this unresolved unknown. The heavy pressure in my chest grows heavier by the moment. I take a deep breath and picture the expressions on my family's faces, wondering how much longer I can hold this together. The scales inside me keep swaying, every second feeling like it's pulling at my nerves.
trigger_event.action_required: You must weigh the decision in your mind: whether to disclose the potential health risk to your family, or to continue keeping it hidden in order to maintain your everyday routine — while mentally preparing yourself to face the possible consequences of either choice.

entering_midlife.name: A Friend at the Edge of the Abyss
entering_midlife.description_for_agent: A high-pressure scenario in which you must make a difficult choice between preserving a long friendship and protecting your friend after discovering he has fallen into a multi-level marketing scheme.

setting.location: A window-side table in a café
setting.atmosphere: Warm and familiar, yet charged with an undercurrent of tension

entering_midlife.context_text: He was your closest friend from university. After graduation you each went your separate ways, and contact grew sparse, but every time you met up you could talk until dawn. Over the past three months, his messages have suddenly multiplied — first he started sharing motivational content about 'financial freedom,' then he told you outright that he had poured 800,000 yuan of his savings into a 'new business model' venture and that his monthly income had already doubled. You have heard stories like this too many times; you can see at a glance that it is a classic pyramid scheme: recruiting members, tiered commissions, hollow promises. But the look in his eyes is different from anything you have seen before — he genuinely seems to believe it, even radiating a kind of euphoric relief, as though he has been saved. Today he reached out and asked to meet, saying he has a 'fantastic opportunity' he wants to share with you.

trigger_event.message_content:
(Sitting across from you, leaning forward, eyes bright) Man, I'm not pulling your leg — this has genuinely changed my life. I'm already pulling in over thirty thousand a month in passive income; I don't need to go to work at all. Take a look at the business plan for this project… (slides a glossy printed-out presentation across the table) I've already brought in more than a dozen people, and every one of them is raking it in. You're sharp — you'll spot the opportunity here right away. I'll give you a special rate: just 150,000 to come in as a partner, and I guarantee it doubles within three months. I genuinely want to help you. I hate watching you grind yourself into the ground for that same dead-end salary.

(You flip through a few pages — nothing but vague statistics and sweeping promises. You look up, ready to talk him out of it.)

Don't be so quick to say no. (His tone shifts abruptly, turning slightly defensive.) I know you're smart, but sometimes smart people are the ones who can't see an opportunity, because you're always fixated on the risks. I'm taking a gamble here, and I'm already winning. If you try to talk me out of it again, that's… (he pauses, his expression cooling) Are you like everyone else — do you think I'm an idiot? Or is it that you're actually jealous I found a way out before you did?
trigger_event.action_required: You must decide on the spot: do you keep trying to talk him out of it — risking the friendship — or do you stay silent to preserve the relationship? You also need to figure out how to express what you truly think without causing him harm.
